k8: The enterprise trust operating system for the AI era.

k24: Traditional IAM is just an access card—controlling who gets through the door. Sanctward is the access card, the surveillance camera and the smart guard in one: it sees what people do inside your apps, what AI agents do behind the scenes, and where your data is flowing.
k32: — A 03:14 login from Tokyo deviates 99% from baseline → triggers STEP-UP MFA, score −42.
k36: Engineers use Copilot, sales use ChatGPT—Sanctward is the first line of defense against sensitive data leaking to external LLMs: the endpoint sees the clipboard, and the Gateway funnels all LLM traffic.
k44: : the endpoint can see, the Gateway can control—the only vendor on the market with both.
k47: Multi-dimensional signals are weighted in milliseconds to dynamically allow, step-up MFA, or deny—seamless to the user, with instant score drops and forced disconnects on anomalies.
k56: Web RDP/SSH/VNC, full session recording, OCR full-text search, JIT time-boxed access—maintainers never touch passwords, and access leaves zero residue on expiry.
k69: Confirm 'who is this, and may they enter'—unify identity governance and multi-factor authentication, bringing every entry's keys under control so every login is accountable.
k71: See 'what happens after entry'—full session recording and content auditing turn privileged actions from a blind spot into something you can replay and prove.
k73: Act 'the moment risk appears'—continuous verification by real-time trust score immediately steps up auth, intercepts, or force-disconnects on anomalies.
k74: One platform, five capabilities on one signal chain
k75: Not five products bolted together—identity feeds trust, devices affect the score, the score drives access, and every action is written to the audit log. Auto-rotating; click to expand.
k77: Federated login, directory sync, instant offboarding
k87: One login for every system—even legacy apps onboard with no modification. Sanctward is itself a complete OIDC Identity Provider.
k88: OIDC / SAML federation + form fill + reverse proxy—90%+ of auth modes can be onboarded
k89: Two-way AD / LDAP sync; offboarded accounts auto-disabled, zero residue
k92: Ensure that 'getting in' always comes with 'seeing what's done.' Maintainers don't need—and shouldn't know—the target system's password.
k93: Web RDP/SSH/VNC with zero client install, or a native client at <15ms latency
k94: Full session recording + OCR full-text search—locate by keyword in seconds
k95: JIT time-boxed access, controlled credential injection, zero residue on expiry
k98: Trust no preset 'safe' environment—every access is evaluated dynamically on current state, in milliseconds, seamless to the user.
k99: Real-time 0–100 trust score: Geo-IP, impossible travel, threat-intel weighting
k100: Behavioral baseline self-learns; deviation drops the score, steps up, or disconnects
k101: Merkle tamper-proof evidence chain keeps audit logs immutable
k105: Every device has a verifiable identity—managed from onboarding to retirement; 99.9% automated onboarding tested across 1,500 endpoints.
k106: Cross-platform installers .msi / .pkg / .deb; private CA configured once, trusted automatically
k107: Device posture (OS, encryption, EDR) feeds the trust score in real time
k108: Fingerprint-drift detection, auto cert renewal, remote removal
k111: Every action is traceable, every regulation reportable. Audit shifts from 'digging through logs afterward' to 'the adapter maps it automatically, generated in one click.'
k112: 100+ event types, before/after deltas, automatic masking of sensitive fields
k114: Compliance adapter auto-maps clauses for ISO 27001 / SOC 2 / FSC
k115: Three things we can do that others can't
k116: We integrate four signals at once—identity events, session footage, in-app content and data flow—forming a proprietary trust dataset that grows richer with use. This is the shared foundation of our three differentiators.
k124: “In the past 7 days, did anyone here paste customer data into ChatGPT?”
k125: 69% of organizations suspect employees use unauthorized GenAI tools; the average company runs 14 AI tools but IT knows only 4–5. Truly governing Shadow AI takes 'two hands' working together—the first on the employee endpoint, seeing the clipboard and which LLM the browser reaches; the second at the traffic choke point, forcing access to approved models only through the enterprise Gateway. Endpoint-only DLP can see but not control; network-only DLP can control but not see. Sanctward is the only vendor with both.
k134: “How many AI agents do we actually have? Who owns them? If something breaks, can we find anyone?”
k135: MCP, function-calling and autonomous workflows mean every company will soon have 'more AI agents than employees.' Yet most of these non-human identities are ghosts—no owner, no permission boundary, no audit trail. Sanctward treats every AI agent as a governable 'employee': issuing a verifiable identity passport (private_key_jwt / Client Credentials), binding an owner, scoping the systems and data it can access, auditing everything, locking on anomalies and revoking with one click. We define the standard for this new category first—followers are 2–3 years behind.
k139: “Give me, for the last 90 days, all evidence of schema changes to the production database.”
k140: Traditional recording is a liability—hours of footage to scrub through afterward. Sanctward turns every session recording into a searchable 'Google for actions.' Endpoint-side recording, OCR full-text indexing and WORM tamper-proof storage let you pinpoint exactly when any action happened—and the session clip—with a single natural-language query. Auditors get a ready-made evidence pack mapped to the clauses, not a pile of raw logs—cutting SOC 2 evidence prep from a week to 30 minutes.
k145: Replace legacy-architecture headaches with zero-trust answers
k146: From VPN and remote access to identity and compliance—six of the most common scenarios, covered by one Sanctward platform.
k148: From 'opening the whole network' to 'opening only authorized apps'
k149: Once you're on a traditional SSL VPN, the entire internal network is wide open to you—and there's no visibility into what happens inside the encrypted tunnel. That's the most common gateway for lateral movement and data leakage.
k151: Over-trust: once connected, you can move laterally to any internal host.
k152: No content visibility: actions inside the encrypted tunnel can't be monitored in real time.
k153: No endpoint control: access is granted without checking device compliance, so stolen credentials walk right in.
k155: Zero-trust per-app authorization; backend locations are hidden, so there's no 'internal network' to traverse.
k156: Real-time trust scoring and continuous re-verification per connection; anomalies drop the score or force a disconnect.
k157: The endpoint agent checks device posture (OS / encryption / EDR) and only allows compliant devices.
k160: , with an OCR full-text index built in real time—so you can replay by keyword and pinpoint any frame in seconds.
k170: Replace remote-access software · harden remote protocols
k171: RDP / VNC / SSH / Telnet — all through one controlled gateway
k172: Installing third-party remote software on endpoints opens a persistent tunnel IT can't see; exposing remote ports directly puts your attack surface on the open network. Both turn 'who connected and what they did' into a black hole.
k173: Persistent endpoint tunnels bypass enterprise controls—who connected and what they did can't be seen or investigated.
k174: If an access code or credential leaks, the session is hijacked—with no recording and no audit trail.
k175: Exposing RDP / VNC / SSH / Telnet ports to the outside creates a direct attack entry point.
k176: All four protocols go through the Sanctward gateway—no third-party software on endpoints, and backend ports stay private.
k177: Full session recording + OCR full-text search + command auditing; controlled credential injection means maintainers never touch plaintext passwords.
k178: JIT time-boxed access with zero residue on expiry; clipboard DLP and SHA-256 integrity checks on file transfers.
k182: One screen — co-watched by many, instantly taken over, fully recorded
k183: Remote collaboration, training, emergency response and audit monitoring often need to 'see the same screen.' Sanctward lets admins instantly share any live RDP / VNC / SSH / Telnet session and switch seamlessly from 'watching' to 'taking over'—the whole process recorded and instantly indexed for search.
k184: Remote help means everyone connects separately, with no shared-screen collaboration—hard to step in when something goes wrong.
k185: In emergencies a senior engineer must take over, but can't instantly take control of the same session.
k186: Monitoring and training sessions aren't recorded—no way to look back, no audit trail.
k187: Multi-Session Shadow: watch any live session in real time, with many people co-watching the same screen.
k188: Switch from 'watch' to 'take over' in one click, letting authorized staff act directly in an emergency.
k189: Full recording + real-time OCR index—who watched, who took over and what they did is all logged and searchable.
k201: Many accounts, many systems, many MFAs — converged into one login
k202: Each system has its own accounts and its own MFA (or none)—management is fragmented, audits have blind spots, and cleanly offboarding accounts in one shot is even harder.
k203: Scattered accounts: a separate credential per system per employee—resets and reclaiming are slow and error-prone for IT.
k204: Inconsistent MFA: some TOTP, some SMS, some none—poor experience and lingering risk.
k205: Legacy systems don't support SSO / MFA and stay outside unified control for the long term.
k206: Form fill / reverse proxy / OIDC · SAML federation onboard 90%+ of auth modes—legacy apps connect without modification.
k207: A built-in IdP provides consistent MFA (TOTP) across all apps, with risk-based step-up.
k208: AD / LDAP sync: disable once on offboarding and access dies everywhere instantly, with consistent conditional access.
k220: Taiwan Cyber Security Act · PDPA · FSC, aligned at once
k221: Taiwan's Cyber Security Management Act and its tiered regulations, the Personal Data Protection Act, and the FSC's remote-maintenance requirements all set clear rules for log retention, access control and audit trails. Sanctward maps directly to these clauses, and its compliance adapter auto-generates auditable evidence.
k223: The Tiered Cyber Security Responsibility regulations require logs kept ≥ 6 months, recording account/permission changes, logins, IPs and data access → 100+ audit events + WORM immutability + Merkle evidence chain, with configurable retention.
k224: High/medium responsibility tiers require MFA and strict access control for critical systems → built-in IdP with consistent MFA (TOTP), fine-grained RBAC, conditional access, instant offboarding.
k225: FSC remote-maintenance / outsourcing audits require connection records and recording → full RDP / SSH / VNC / Telnet recording + OCR command search + controlled credential injection + JIT time-boxing.
k226: PDPA Article 27 security measures and access trails → end-to-end logging of access to personal-data systems, automatic masking of sensitive fields, fully traceable evidence.
k238: Going global means simultaneously meeting international frameworks' access-control, audit-logging and incident-response requirements. Sanctward's controls map directly to each framework's specific clauses, generating an evidence pack in one click.
k239: ISO/IEC 27001:2022 A.8.5 secure authentication, A.8.15 logging, A.8.16 monitoring, A.5.15 access control → unified SSO / MFA, 100+ event logs, real-time monitoring and trust scoring.
k240: SOC 2 Trust Services CC6 access control, CC7 monitoring & incident response → RBAC + MFA, WORM audit trail, behavioral-baseline anomaly detection.
k241: EU GDPR Art. 32 security of processing, Art. 30 records of processing, Art. 33 72-hour breach notification → encryption and access control, complete audit trails, real-time anomaly alerts to support notification timelines.
k242: EU NIS2 Art. 21 (MFA, access control, logging) and Art. 23 incident reporting → enforced MFA / continuous verification, immutable logs, investigation evidence chain.
k243: US HIPAA §164.312 technical safeguards (access control, audit controls, authentication) → unique identity, session recording and auditing, strong authentication.
